extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Physical Twin (PT) is a thermal incubator system comprising an insulated styrofoam container (0.03 m³ volume), a heatbed (214×214×3 mm) for uniform heating, a fan (0.02 m³/s airflow) for air circulation, two internal temperature sensors (DS18S20, -55°C to +125°C, 0.5°C accuracy), and a Raspberry Pi 4 Model B controller. The system regulates temperature within the box regardless of content, with a heatbed capable of delivering 100W power to raise air temperature from 293K (20°C) to 300K (26.85°C) in under 2 seconds. The PT operates as a Cyber-Physical System (CPS) with physical components (insulated container, heatbed, fan) and cyber components (controller, sensors).
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin (DT) provides monitoring (real-time temperature tracking via sensors), prediction (thermal inertia analysis using two simulation models with 2 and 4 free parameters), optimization (system performance tuning), control (temperature regulation via Raspberry Pi controller), and visualization (data representation for analysis). It enables reconfigurability, safety assurance, and reduced maintenance through advanced analytics and fault detection algorithms. The DT synchronizes with the PT via RabbitMQ for real-time data exchange and simulation of PT behavior under operating conditions.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Tools and technologies include: Raspberry Pi 4 Model B (controller with GPIO for sensors/fan/heatbed), DS18S20 temperature sensors (1-Wire bus with 64-bit serial codes), MOSFETs and optocouplers for electrical isolation, PCB schematic for hardware integration, RabbitMQ (open-source message broker for communication server), simulation models (2-parameter and 4-parameter thermal models), and open-source repository (GitHub: https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator) for hardware/software specifications. The DT leverages calibration algorithms, state estimators, and supervisory controllers for model updating and system monitoring.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Real-time synchronization between physical and digital components via RabbitMQ message passing (latency < 2 seconds for temperature control). Kalman filter state estimation operates in real-time with sub-second updates. Calibration experiments generate data trajectories for time-scale approximation, with model B predicting heatbed inertia with higher temporal fidelity.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: Two distinct digital twin models (A/B) with differing parameter sets (2 vs 4 free parameters). Centralized architecture via Raspberry Pi controller coordinating sensors/actuators. Hierarchical structure includes physical twin (incubator), communication layer (RabbitMQ), and model instances (A/B) with coordinated calibration workflows.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Orchestrated system integrating: 1) RabbitMQ as message broker for real-time data exchange, 2) Raspberry Pi as central controller with GPIO interface, 3) PCB-based sensor/actuator network, 4) Kalman filter for state estimation, 5) Calibration framework for model parameter refinement. Resource management includes power supply coordination and 1-Wire bus multiplexing for temperature sensors.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Interoperability via AMQP protocol (RabbitMQ) for cross-system data exchange. Integration with external monitoring tools through time-series databases. Open-source repository enables third-party extensions. Industry 4.0 alignment through real-time simulation and self-adaptation loops with external system dependencies.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Digital Twin computational models include behavioral (differential equations for dynamics, Kalman filters for state estimation) and analytical (energy flow equations Q=cmΔT) models. Data sources: sensors (temperature sensors, 1-Wire bus) and physical twin (PT) operational data. Data management uses time series databases for real-time visualization, RabbitMQ for message brokering, and calibration algorithms to update model parameters (e.g., C_air=616.56 J/K, G_box=0.65 J/K for Model A). Models B includes additional parameters (C_heater=33.65 J/K, G_heater=0.87 J/K) for higher fidelity. Models are integrated with simulation algorithms for what-if scenarios and optimization.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuators include heatbed (controlled via MOSFETs with optocouplers for isolation) and fan. Control mechanisms: bang-bang controller with parameters LL (lower temp limit), UL (upper temp limit), H (heating duration), C (waiting duration). Remote control via Raspberry Pi GPIO through low-level drivers. Safety constraints: safe protocol in low-level drivers to prevent overheating. Actuation ranges: heatbed power management (4A via MOSFETs), fan control for temperature regulation.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors: DS18S20 High-Precision 1-Wire Digital Thermometers (3 units, 0.5°C accuracy from -10°C to +85°C). Spatial distribution: deployed within styrofoam box with fan for uniform temperature distribution. Sampling frequency: continuous temperature data collection. Data transmission: 1-Wire bus with unique 64-bit serial codes per sensor, data uploaded to RabbitMQ server. Precision: ±0.5°C accuracy, 1-Wire bus supports multiple sensors on single GPIO.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity requirements: Model B (4 parameters) outperforms Model A (2 parameters) in predicting heatbed thermal inertia. Validation methods: calibration experiments (e.g., C_air=486.12 J/K, G_box=0.856 J/K for Model B), comparison of simulated vs. real PT behavior. Uncertainty sources: sensor accuracy (±0.5°C), model assumptions (e.g., uniform temperature distribution). Error correction: Kalman filter for state estimation, anomaly detection to trigger recalibration. Quality assurance: open-source hardware/software for reproducibility, parameter tuning via co-simulations.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data flows from physical sensors (DS18S20 temperature sensors, fan airflow measurements) to digital twin via RabbitMQ message broker. Data types include temperature readings (°C, 0.5°C accuracy), airflow volume (0.02 m³/s), and power metrics (100W). Communication protocol: MQTT/RabbitMQ with message serialization. Transmission frequency: Continuous data streaming with event triggers for temperature anomalies. Data preprocessing includes calibration (e.g., C_air=616.56 J/K, G_box=0.65 J/K). Data integration mechanisms involve time-series databases for dashboard visualization.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands (fan speed, heatbed power) are transmitted via RabbitMQ to Raspberry Pi GPIO. Command formats: JSON-based messages with actuator control parameters. Validation includes parameter range checks (e.g., 12V/24V fan operation). Feedback systems use temperature sensors for closed-loop control. Conflict resolution prioritizes safety thresholds (e.g., overheating prevention). Command execution confirmation via message acknowledgments in RabbitMQ.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Network infrastructure uses RabbitMQ (AMQP protocol) for bidirectional communication. Network topology: Edge (Raspberry Pi) ↔ Cloud (DT dashboard). Bandwidth requirements: 100Mbit/s for real-time data streaming. Latency: <500ms for control signal propagation. Reliability mechanisms: Message persistence and retries. Security protocols: TLS encryption for data in transit. Redundancy: Dual temperature sensors for fault tolerance.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Deployment on edge (Raspberry Pi 4 Model B) with cloud integration for visualization. Computational requirements: 4-core ARM Cortex-A53 @ 1.5GHz. Storage: Time-series database for 100+ hours of sensor data. Scalability: Modular architecture supports additional sensors. Deployment models: Hybrid edge-cloud. Availability: 99.9% uptime via redundant sensor networks and fail-safe shutdown protocols.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin (DT) lifecycle includes development stages (modeling, simulation, calibration), deployment (integration with Physical Twin (PT) via sensors and data collection), and operational phases (monitoring, optimization, and self-adaptation). Transition management involves updating models with calibrated parameters (e.g., Model A to Model B) and recalibrating during system changes (e.g., adding new objects). Lifecycle governance includes real-time data ingestion, Kalman filter-based state estimation, and what-if simulations for continuous improvement.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The DT development uses methodologies like simulation model calibration (e.g., two-parameter vs. four-parameter models), RabbitMQ for real-time communication between controller and low-level drivers, and version control via model parameter updates. Evolution strategies include recalibrating Model B with new experimental data, reconfiguring Kalman filters, and iterative optimization of controller parameters. Testing validates models against real-world data (e.g., temperature trajectories) and ensures interoperability through open-source tools.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Decision support includes real-time visualization of incubator status via dashboards, anomaly detection using Kalman filters to estimate heatbed temperatures, and what-if simulations for controller optimization. Analytics processes involve correlating sensor data with model predictions (e.g., Model B) and self-adaptation loops that reconfigure controllers based on detected plant behavior changes. User interaction is via dashboard visualization, with confidence measures derived from Kalman filter residuals and calibration accuracy.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Compliance includes open-source tools (RabbitMQ, DS18S20 sensors) and adherence to IoT protocols for communication. Industry standards are implied through CPS integration (e.g., IEEE 1471 for architecture) and interoperability via 1-Wire bus for temperature sensors. Certification requirements are not explicitly stated, but the use of standardized hardware (Raspberry Pi, optocouplers) and protocols (MQTT/RabbitMQ) ensures compatibility with broader digital twin ecosystems.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

### **6 Summary**


We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. Hardware,
software, and datasets are available online [6] . Using this example to set the terminology,
we proceeded to demystify the DT concept and its goals.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re

specifications of the case study.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.

We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope

The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.

wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

This constitutes our future work. Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables **Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series database, and plot the data into a dashboard. **On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented in [15]. **Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature. **What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary**

**2.2.3** **The Controller**


The controller used in the case study is a comparable to a bang-bang controller, which
a small difference described below. The control strategy is executed after receiving the

temperature measurements.
The principle of bang-bang control is that when a reference temperature is given
to the controller, the controller issues a heating signal turning on the heatbed until the
measured temperature from sensors reaches the reference temperature. Because of the
delayed effect of the temperature, the controller needs to wait after each actuation, to
make sure the temperature does not rise too much. Figure 8 shows the state chart of the

the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided

DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with

This constitutes our future work. Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables **Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series database, and plot the data into a dashboard. **On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented in [15]. **Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature. **What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary**

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.

We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary**

the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.
